coredata.dc:title: Enhancing leachate management with antibacterial nanocomposites incorporating plant-based carbon dots and Satureja Khuzestanica essential oils
coredata.dc:creator: Rezaei, Ali
Monfared-Hajishirkiaee, Reza
Hosseinzadeh-Moghaddam, Setareh
Behzadi, Manouchehr
Shahangian, S. Shirin
coredata.dc:description: Landfill leachate, a complex mixture of pollutants, poses a significant environmental hazard. This study reports the synthesis and characterization of superabsorbent nanocomposites (SANs) designed for enhanced performance in waste management applications. SANs were prepared using carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) and sodium polyacrylate (SPA) as the main components, carbon dots (CDs) to improve absorption, and Satureja Khuzestanica essential oil (SEO) for antibacterial performance. The results demonstrated that the addition of CDs significantly increased the absorption capacity and liquid retention of the samples, with a water absorption capacity reaching up to 8621 %. Furthermore, the samples exhibited high mechanical strength, with tensile strength improving by over 100 % in the presence of CDs. The inclusion of SEO provided strong antibacterial activity against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus, with inhibition zones measuring up to 26 mm. These SANs, with their high absorption capacity, mechanical robustness, and antibacterial properties, show great potential for improving waste management practices, particularly in leachate absorption strategies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Superabsorbent nanocomposites
Landfill leachate
Sodium polyacrylate
Plant carbon dots
Antibacterial performance

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors extend their utmost gratitude to Polyean Technology Inc for their invaluable financial and technical support in facilitating this groundbreaking research. Their contribution will undoubtedly accelerate knowledge development and pave the way for solutions to important challenges. Also, the authors express their gratitude to the University of Guilan for the support of this project.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic synthesis method of fabrication steps of superabsorbent nanocomposite samples.
Characterization of CDs: (a) DLS results of CDs, (b) XRD results of CDs, and (c) FTIR results of CDs.

SEM images of the super composite samples. (a) CMC/SPA sample (The scale bars: 200 μm and 100 μm), (b) CMC/SPA/CD sample (The scale bars: 200 μm and 100 μm), (c) CMC/SPA/SEO sample (The scale bars: 50 μm and 5 μm), (d) CMC/SPA/CD/SEO sample (The scale bars: 10 μm and 10 μm), (e) FTIR results of CMC/SPA, CMC/SPA/CD, CMC/SPA/SEO, and CMC/SPA/CD/SEO samples, (f) Porosity of CMC/SPA, CMC/SPA/CD, CMC/SPA/SEO, and CMC/SPA/CD/SEO samples. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3, ANOVA test, **p < 0.01, ****p < 0.0001.

(a) Tensile strength, (b) Bending stress, and (c) Elongation at break of CMC/SPA, CMC/SPA/CD, CMC/SPA/SEO, and CMC/SPA/CD/SEO samples, (d) Images of flexibility of CMC/SPA/CD/SEO sample. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3, ANOVA test, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001.

Absorption rate measurement of CMC/SPA, CMC/SPA/CD, CMC/SPA/SEO, and CMC/SPA/CD/SEO samples. (a) Water, (b) NaOH 0.1 M, (c) HCl 0.1 M, (d) NaCl 0.1 M, (e) NaCl 0.5 M, (f) NaCl 1 M, (g) Images of CMC/SPA/CD/SEO sample water absorption. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3, ANOVA test.

(a) Water retention of CMC/SPA, CMC/SPA/CD, CMC/SPA/SEO, and CMC/SPA/CD/SEO samples, (b) Biodegradability of CMC/SPA, CMC/SPA/CD, CMC/SPA/SEO, and CMC/SPA/CD/SEO samples. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3, ANOVA test, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001.

(a) Images of the inhibition zone of CMC/SPA, CMC/SPA/CD, CMC/SPA/SEO, and CMC/SPA/CD/SEO samples against E. coli and S. aureus, (b) The results of the antibacterial performance of CMC/SPA, CMC/SPA/CD, CMC/SPA/SEO, and CMC/SPA/CD/SEO samples.Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3, ANOVA test, *p < 0.05, ****p < 0.0001.
Chemical composition of Satureja Khuzestanica essential oil.

head.ce:title: Enhancing leachate management with antibacterial nanocomposites incorporating plant-based carbon dots and Satureja Khuzestanica essential oils

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology, Shahid Beheshti University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Environment, Tehran University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Environment, Tehran University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biology, Faculty of Sciences, University of Guilan, Rasht, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biology, Faculty of Sciences, University of Guilan

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biology, Faculty of Sciences, University of Guilan, Rasht, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Landfill leachate, a complex mixture of pollutants, poses a significant environmental hazard. This study reports the synthesis and characterization of superabsorbent nanocomposites (SANs) designed for enhanced performance in waste management applications. SANs were prepared using carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) and sodium polyacrylate (SPA) as the main components, carbon dots (CDs) to improve absorption, and Satureja Khuzestanica essential oil (SEO) for antibacterial performance. The results demonstrated that the addition of CDs significantly increased the absorption capacity and liquid retention of the samples, with a water absorption capacity reaching up to 8621 %. Furthermore, the samples exhibited high mechanical strength, with tensile strength improving by over 100 % in the presence of CDs. The inclusion of SEO provided strong antibacterial activity against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus, with inhibition zones measuring up to 26 mm. These SANs, with their high absorption capacity, mechanical robustness, and antibacterial properties, show great potential for improving waste management practices, particularly in leachate absorption strategies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Landfill leachate is a significant environmental concern due to its complex composition of organic and inorganic pollutants, which can contaminate soil, water, and air if not properly managed [1]. Leachate contains hazardous substances such as heavy metals, organic compounds, ammonia, and various pathogens, which pose serious risks to ecosystems and human health [2]. The improper management of leachate can lead to groundwater contamination, surface water pollution, and the release of toxic gases, exacerbating environmental degradation [3]. Several techniques have been developed to treat landfill leachate, such as biological, chemical, and physical methods [2]. However, these conventional methods have limitations, such as high cost, low efficiency, and secondary pollution. Therefore, there is a need eco-friendly materials that can effectively remove pollutants from leachate and enhance its quality [4]. Given these challenges, adopting a sustainable approach for leachate management offers several advantages. Sustainable technologies that prioritize sustainability focus on minimizing secondary pollution, reducing energy consumption, and utilizing environmentally responsible materials [5]. Such approaches not only mitigate environmental harm but also align with global efforts to achieve sustainable waste management practices [6]. Recent studies highlight the potential of using natural and biodegradable materials in leachate treatment, which can provide effective pollutant removal while being environmentally benign [7]. Superabsorbent nanocomposites (SANs) are materials that combine the high water absorption and retention capacity of superabsorbent polymers (SAPs) with the enhanced mechanical, thermal, and functional properties of nanofillers [8]. SANs have attracted considerable attention in recent years due to their potential applications in various fields such as agriculture, hygiene, medicine, and environmental еnginееring [9]. Among the different types of nanofillers, carbon-based materials such as carbon dots (CDs) have shown promising results in improving the performance of polymers [10,11]. CDs are nanosized carbon particles with fluorescent properties that can be derived from various natural sources such as plants, fruits, and biomass [12,13]. Plant-derived carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as promising nanomaterials for environmental applications due to their unique properties [14]. These nanoparticles, synthesized from renewable biomass, exhibit biocompatibility, chemical stability, and photoluminescent properties, making them suitable for a wide range of environmental applications [11]. Specifically, the high surface area, tunable surface functionalization, and ability to act as electron donors or acceptors enable plant-derived CDs to interact effectively with various pollutants, facilitating their degradation or removal [15]. Furthermore, plant-derived CDs have shown strong antioxidant and antimicrobial activities, which are particularly advantageous in environmental remediation contexts, where they can enhance the breakdown of contaminants and inhibit the growth of harmful microorganisms [16]. Another type of natural additive that can improve the functionality of SANs is essential oil (EO), which is a volatile liquid extracted from aromatic plants EO can provide antimicrobial, еffеcts to SANs, as well as modulate their swelling behavior and rеlеasе kinetics [17,18].

In addition, Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) is a biodegradable and biocompatible polysaccharide that can form hydrogels with SPA by physical cross-linking [19]. Sodium polyacrylate) SPA (is a synthetic polymer that can absorb large amounts of water due to its carboxylate groups [20]. CMC and SPA not only provide robust water absorption and retention properties but also serve as effective matrices for incorporating nanomaterials, enhancing the overall functionality and performance of superabsorbent composites in environmental applications [21]. Satureja Khuzestanica essential oil)SEO(is a plant-derived EO that has strong antimicrobial and antioxidant properties [22]. SEO is extracted from the leaves of Suturae Khuzestan, a medicinal plant native to Iran, which is used as an herbal remedy for various ailments such as inflammation, pain, infection, and diabetes [23].

In this research, we present the synthesis and characterization of four SANs, combining CMC and sodium polyacrylate (SPA) as the superabsorbent polymer matrix, with carbon dots (CDs) as nanofillers and Satureja Khuzestanica essential oil (SEO) as an antibacterial agent. The CDs were synthesized by pyrolysis of plant biomass and characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, dynamic light scattering (DLS), and X-ray diffraction (XRD). The morphology of the SANs was investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and the functional groups was analyzed by FTIR spectroscopy. The porosity, mechanical properties, biodegradability, liquid absorption and retention, and antimicrobial properties of the SANs were also valuated. While CMC and SPA are well-known for their water absorption capabilities, the integration of plant-derived CDs and SEO introduces significant enhancements in both the functional properties and environmental compatibility of the resulting nanocomposites. Unlike conventional materials, our approach leverages the synergistic effects of CDs and SEO to not only improve mechanical strength and water retention but also to impart potent antibacterial activity. This study is pioneering in its use of plant-sourced CDs and SEO within SANs, offering a sustainable, multifunctional material that addresses critical challenges in waste management and environmental remediation. The comprehensive characterization and performance evaluation underscore the innovative potential of these SANs, positioning them as a solution for advanced leachate management strategies.

CDs were prepared using the pyrolysis method and the natural precursor of Sambucus nigra fruit. The fruit of this plant was collected from the grasslands of Gilan province. 1.5 g of the desired plant material was ground using a Chinese mortar. The sample was placed inside a glass beaker and then placed in a laboratory oven for 6 h at a temperature of 180 °C. After the desired time, the black powder was separated after cooling to room temperature. In the next step, larger particles were removed with a horizontal sieve. Then, using a ball mill, the desired particles were crushed into smaller particles. Finally, the resulting black powder was stored at 25 °C for further analysis.

Satureja Khuzestanica leaves were collected from the natural habitats of the Khuzestan province of Iran and dried for 3 days at room temperature. Then it was powdered with a laboratory mill at a speed of 5000 rpm for 5 min. The powdered leaves were distilled using a Cloninger apparatus for 4 h. Finally, the essential oil of Satureja Khuzestanica was collected and stored in sealed vials at 4 °C until use.

Dynamic light scattering analysis (DLS Horiba) was used to determine the size of CDs and their distribution in liquids. The XRD analysis was performed with a high-performance diffractometer (XRD, PW1730, Philips, Netherlands) using Cu Kα radiation (λ= 1.540598 Å) at 40 kV and 30 mA. FTIR analysis (Bruker, Germany) was performed in the range of 400–4000 cm −1 to identify the functional groups of CDs.

The chemical composition of SEO was analyzed by Gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC-MS) (Agilent 7890 A). The SEO sample was diluted in n-hexane and injected into a capillary column with helium as the carrier gas. The oven temperature was gradually increased from 50 °C to 300 °C to vaporize the sample and separate the compounds. The injection and detector temperatures were 220 °C and 300 °C, respectively, to prevent condensation or degradation of the sample. The GC-MS chromatogram showed the different compounds in the SEO sample, which were identified by comparing their mass spectra with libraries or standards.

The surface features of samples were examined by a scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Tescan, VEGA3, Czech Republic). The samples were coated with a thin layer of gold before the SEM analysis to enhance their conductivity.

FTIR spectroscopy (Tensor 27, Bruker, Germany) was used to analyze the chemical groups in the samples. The sample was mixed with pure KBr and a thin tablet of sponge nanocomposites was made. The infrared absorption peaks were measured at room temperature by FTIR in the range of 400–4000 cm−1.

The porosity of the samples was measured by a liquid displacement method. The samples were weighed before and after soaking in absolute ethanol until no further weight change was observed. The porosity was calculated from the weight difference and the volume of the samples, using the following formula:
(1)
Porosity % =
( m 2 − m 1 )
ρ V
× 100
where m1 and m2 are the initial and final weights of the samples, ρ is the density of absolute ethanol, and v is the initial volume of the samples.

The mechanical properties of the SANs were evaluated using a Universal Testing Machine (UTM) (SANTAM, STM-20, Iran) equipped with a 5 kN load cell. The SANs were cut into rectangular strips measuring 10 cm in length and 2 cm in width, with thickness measured using a digital caliper (accuracy ± 0.01 mm). Prior to testing, the samples were conditioned at 25°C and 50 % relative humidity for 24 h. Tensile strength tests were conducted according to the American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM) D638 standard. Each sample was clamped between the grips of the UTM, and the test was carried out at a crosshead speed of 10 mm/min until the sample reached its breaking point. The maximum force applied before failure was recorded, and the tensile strength was calculated using the formula:
(2)
Tensile Strength ( MPa ) =
Maximum Force ( N )
Cross − sectional Area ( mm 2 )

Bending stress tests were conducted according to ASTM D790 using a three-point bending configuration. The samples were placed on two supports with a specific support span (L), and a load was applied at the midpoint. The bending stress (σ) was calculated using the following formula:
(3)
Bending Stress σ =
3 × F × L
2 × b × d 2

Elongation at break was measured during the tensile testing. The percentage elongation was calculated as the ratio of the extension of the sample at break to the original gauge length:
(4)
Elongation at Break ( % ) =
Extension at Break ( mm )
Initial Gauge Length ( mm )
×
100
Where:
F is the applied force at the midpoint (N),
L is the support span (mm),
b is the width of the sample (mm),
d is the thickness of the sample (mm).
Each mechanical test was performed in triplicate to ensure the reliability and accuracy of the results.

The absorption rate of the nanocomposite samples was measured under various pH conditions and ionic strengths to simulate the absorption conditions of liquids with different pH and ion concentrations. This experimental setup aimed to mimic real-world scenarios where nanocomposites are exposed to liquids with diverse pH and ion concentrations, such as in leachate absorption applications. For the neutral condition, distilled water was utilized as the solvent to mimic a neutral pH environment. In the acidic condition, 0.1 M hydrochloric acid (HCl) was employed to replicate an acidic pH environment, while for the basic condition, 0.1 M sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was utilized to represent a basic pH environment. A digital pH meter was utilized to monitor and adjust the pH of the solution as necessary by adding small amounts of HCl or NaOH. To further assess the absorption behavior of the nanocomposites, the samples were subjected to three sodium chloride (NaCl) solutions with varying concentrations (0.1 M, 0.5 M, and 1.0 M) to simulate environments with different ionic strengths. For each condition, a 100 mL beaker was filled with 50 mL of the solvent and placed on a magnetic stirrer. A magnetic stir bar was added to the beaker and the stirring speed was set to 300 rpm. A 0.1 g sample of each nanocomposite was weighed and added to the beaker. The initial mass of the sample was recorded as W0. The sample was allowed to absorb the solvent for 10, 30, 60, 90, and 120 min. At each time interval, the sample was removed from the beaker, blotted with a filter paper to remove excess solvent, and weighed. The final mass of the sample was recorded as Wt. The absorption rate measurement was repeated three times for each sample to ensure accuracy and reliability. The absorption rate of the sample was calculated using the following formula:
(5)
Absorption ratio
%
=
W t
−
W 0
W 0
×
100

The water retention of the samples was measured in the same conditions as the liquid absorption test. For each condition, a 100 mL beaker was filled with 50 mL of the water and placed on a magnetic stirrer. A 0.1 g sample of each nanocomposite was weighed and added to the beaker. The samples were allowed to absorb the solvent for 120 min. After 120 min, the sample was removed from the beaker, blotted with a filter paper to remove excess solvent, weighed, and recorded as W0. The water retention rate of the samples was measured after certain time intervals and recorded as Wt. The experiment was repeated three times for each sample. The water retention measurement of the samples was calculated using the following formula:
(6)
Water retention ratio
%
=
Wt
W 0
×
100

The biodegradability of the samples was evaluated by soil burial test according to the ASTM D5988–18 standard [24]. The samples were cut into small pieces (approximately 1 cm × 1 cm) and weighed before burying them in plastic pots filled with garden soil. The soil had a pH of 7.2, a moisture content of 15 %, and an organic matter content of 2.5 %. The pots were kept in a greenhouse at 25 °C and 60 % relative humidity for 50 days. The soil was watered regularly to maintain the moisture content. At the end of the test period, the samples were retrieved from the soil, washed with distilled water, dried at 60 °C for 24 h, and weighed again. The weight loss percentage of the samples was calculated as follows:
(7)
Degradation ratio ( % )
=
W 0
− W t
W 0
×
100
where W0 is the initial weight of the sample and Wt is the final weight of the sample after 50 days.

The antibacterial performance of samples was tested by using the agar disk diffusion method. Two types of bacteria were used in this study: Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) (gram-positive) and Escherichia coli (E. coli) (gram-negative). At first, after preparing the Mueller-Hinton agar culture medium, 100 μL of diluted bacterial suspension were inoculated on the culture medium with a density of 1.5 ×106 CFU/mL. Then, 5 mm discs of samples were sterilized under UV light for 20 min and placed on the culture medium. Ciprofloxacin antibiotic discs were used as the control group. The samples were incubated for 24 h at 37 °C and the antimicrobial activity was measured by the diameter of the inhibition zones. The experiment was repeated three times to reduce errors.

The data is presented in a standardized format that includes the mean ± standard deviation (SD) for each group. The ANOVA test was employed to compare the means of the groups, and any observed differences were considered statistically significant only if the p < 0.05.

The DLS spectrum shows the size distribution of CDs in a solution based on the intensity of scattered light. The spectrum has a single peak at around 3 nm, which indicates that the CDs have a narrow size distribution and a high degree of monodispersity. The small size and uniformity of the CDs are attributed to the pyrolysis method, which involves rapid heating and cooling of biomass in an inert atmosphere ( Fig. 2a) [25]. As seen in Fig. 2 b, the XRD spectrum of CDs shows a broad peak at around 20°, which indicates the presence of amorphous carbon structures. The peak corresponds to the (002) plane of graphite, which reflects the interlayer spacing of the sp2-hybridized carbon atoms. The broadness of the peak suggests that the CDs have a small crystallite size and a high degree of disorder. The absence of other peaks implies that the CDs are mostly composed of carbon and do not contain any metal or metal oxide impurities. The FTIR peak of CDs synthesized by pyrolysis showed that the nanomaterial has a complex and heterogeneous structure with various functional groups and bonds. The peak at 3210 cm−1 corresponds to the O-H stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups, indicating surface hydrophilic groups on the CDs. The peak at 2913 cm−1 is attributed to the C-H stretching vibration of aliphatic hydrocarbons, which suggests the existence of sp3 hybridized carbon atoms in the CDs. The peak at 1660 cm−1 is assigned to the CO stretching vibration of carbonyl groups, which implies the formation of oxygen-containing functional groups on the CDs. The peak at 1585 cm−1 is related to the CC stretching vibration of aromatic rings, which indicates the presence of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in the CDs. The peak at 1394 cm−1 is due to the C-N stretching vibration of amine groups, which reveals the incorporation of nitrogen atoms in the CDs [26]. The peak at 1286 cm−1 is ascribed to the C-O stretching vibration of ether groups, which demonstrates the involvement of oxygen atoms in the CDs [27]. The peak at 1021 cm−1 is associated with the C-O-C stretching vibration of ester groups, which shows the formation of ester bonds between oxygen-containing functional groups on the CDs (Fig. 2c). The FTIR analysis revealed the presence of hydroxyl, carbonyl, amine, ether, and ester groups on the surface of the CDs, as well as aliphatic and aromatic hydrocarbons in the core. These features are attributed to the incorporation of oxygen and nitrogen atoms in the CDs and the formation of sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon atoms [28].

Table 1 reveals that the SEO is rich in monoterpenes and sesquiterpenes, which are volatile organic compounds with various biological activities. In total, 98.21 % of the compounds in SEO were identified. The most abundant compound in SEO is carvacrol, which accounts for 93.21 % of the total composition. Carvacrol is a phenolic monoterpene that has strong antimicrobial, antioxidant, and anti-inflammatory properties.

The SEM images of the four superabsorbent nanocomposite samples are shown in Fig. 3a. The samples were prepared by mixing SPA and CMC as the main components and adding plant CDs and SEO as the functional additives. The surface morphology of the samples reveals the effect of adding CDs and SEO on the structure and porosity of the nanocomposites. The CMC/SPA shows a smooth surface with some small cracks, indicating a dense and homogeneous network of cross-linked polymers (Fig. 3a). The CMC/SPA/CD shows a rougher surface with more cracks, suggesting that the incorporation of CDs increased the surface area and porosity of the nanocomposite (Fig. 3b). The CMC/SPA/SEO shows a surface with many small, jagged protrusions, implying that the addition of SEO altered the surface texture and created more irregularities (Fig. 3c). The CMC/SPA/CD/SEO shows a similar surface morphology to the CMC/SPA/SEO sample, but with larger and more pronounced protrusions, indicating that the combination of CDs and SEO enhanced the effect of creating more surface roughness and heterogeneity (Fig. 3d). The different surface morphologies of the samples may have implications for their water absorption capacity, mechanical strength, biodegradability, and antibacterial activity.

The FTIR spectra of the four samples of superabsorbent nanocomposite are shown in Fig. 3 e. The FTIR spectra show that incorporating CDs and SEO into the CMC/SPA matrix did not introduce any new functional groups, but rather caused some changes in the peak positions and intensities. These changes indicate some interactions between the components of the nanocomposite. The most noticeable change is the shift of the O-H stretching peak from 3291 cm−1 in CMC/SPA to 3276 cm−1 in CMC/SPA/CD, 3268 cm−1 in CMC/SPA/SEO, and 3274 cm−1 in CMC/SPA/CD/SEO. This shift suggests that the hydrogen bonding between the hydroxyl groups of CMC and SPA was disrupted by the introduction of CD and SEO, which may have formed new hydrogen bonds with the CMC/SPA matrix. The O-H stretching peak also became broader and less intense in the presence of CD and SEO, indicating a more complex and diverse hydrogen bonding network in the nanocomposite. Another significant change is the increase of the CO stretching peak intensity from 1544 cm−1 in CMC/SPA to 1549 cm−1 in CMC/SPA/CD, 1553 cm−1 in CMC/SPA/SEO, and 1560 cm−1 in CMC/SPA/CD/SEO. This increase suggests that the carbonyl groups of SPA were involved in some interactions with CDs and SEO, possibly through electrostatic or van der Waals forces. The CO stretching peak also shifted slightly to higher wavenumbers, indicating a slight increase in the bond strength of the carbonyl groups. The other peaks showed minor changes in position and intensity, which could be attributed to the different molecular weights and structures of the samples [29]. The C-O-C asymmetric and symmetric stretching peaks shifted slightly to lower wavenumbers in the presence of CDs and SEO, indicating a slight decrease in the bond strength of the ether linkages. The C-N stretching peak also shifted slightly to higher wavenumbers, indicating a slight increase in the bond strength of the amide linkages [30]. The FTIR results demonstrate that the synthesis of superabsorbent nanocomposite by incorporating CDs and SEO into the CMC/SPA matrix was successful and did not alter the main functional groups of the polymer. However, the results also reveal some subtle changes in the peak positions and intensities, which suggest some interactions between the components of the nanocomposite.

The porosity rate is an important parameter that affects the absorption capacity of the nanocomposite. The higher the porosity rate, the more water molecules can enter and occupy the pores of the nanocomposite. The results of the porosity rate measurements of the four samples of superabsorbent nanocomposite are shown in Fig. 3 f. The results show that the addition of plant CDs and SEO to the CMC and SPA base significantly increased the porosity rate of the nanocomposite. The CMC/SPA/CD sample had the highest porosity rate of 94.1 %, which was 28.8 % higher than the CMC/SPA sample. This indicates that the CDs, which were synthesized by the pyrolysis method, created more pores in the nanocomposite structure. The CMC/SPA/SEO sample had a porosity rate of 84.3 %, which was 19 % higher than the CMC/SPA sample. This suggests that the SEO also contributed to the formation of pores in the nanocomposite. The CMC/SPA/CD/SEO sample had a porosity rate of 86.6 % which implies that the combination of CDs and SEO had a significant positive impact on the porosity rate of the nanocomposite. The results indicate that the porosity rate of the superabsorbent nanocomposite can be enhanced by adding CDs and SEO to the CMC and SPA base. This can improve the absorption capacity of the nanocomposite, which is desirable for applications such as agriculture, and environmental engineering [31].

The water retention capacity of the samples was analyzed after the 25-day period ( Fig. 6a). The results showed that the CMC/SPA/CD sample had the highest water retention capacity at 41.6 %, followed by the CMC/SPA/CD/SEO sample at 32.5 %. The CMC/SPA sample had the lowest water retention capacity at 24.7 %. The CMC/SPA/SEO sample had a water retention capacity of 18.6 %. The addition of CDs and SEO had a significant impact on the water retention capacity of the samples. The CMC/SPA/CD sample, which contained both CDs and SEO, had a higher water retention capacity than the CMC/SPA sample, which did not contain these additives. This suggests that the presence of CDs may have improved the water retention capacity of the nanocomposite by enhancing the cross-linking density and hydrophilicity of the polymer chains [41]. The CMC/SPA/SEO sample, which contained only SEO, had a lower water retention capacity than the CMC/SPA sample, which may be due to the hydrophobic nature of SEO [42]. In addition, it may be because the essential oil interfered with the cross-linking of the CMC and SPA, resulting in a weaker network structure [43]. The CMC/SPA/CD/SEO sample, which contained both CDs and SEO, had a lower water retention capacity than the CMC/SPA/CD sample, which may be due to the antagonistic effect of SEO on the CD-induced cross-linking. The results indicate that the optimal composition of the superabsorbent nanocomposite for maximizing the water retention capacity is CMC/SPA/CD, which can be used for various applications.

The results of the biodegradability test showed that the nanocomposite samples had different weight loss percentages after 50 days of exposure to soil. As depicted in Fig. 6b, the CMC/SPA sample had the lowest weight loss percentage of 61.7 %, indicating that it was the least biodegradable among the four samples. The CMC/SPA/CD sample had the highest weight loss percentage of 76.9 %, suggesting that it was the most biodegradable sample. The addition of CDs to the CMC/SPA matrix enhanced the biodegradability of the nanocomposite by increasing the surface area and porosity of the material, as well as providing more sites for microbial degradation. The CMC/SPA/SEO sample had a weight loss percentage of 42.7 %, which was lower than that of the CMC/SPA sample. This indicated that the incorporation of SEO reduced the biodegradability of the nanocomposite by acting as an antimicrobial agent that inhibited the growth of soil microorganisms [44]. The CMC/SPA/CD/SEO sample had a weight loss percentage of 65.9 %, which was higher than that of the CMC/SPA/SEO sample but lower than that of the CMC/SPA/CD sample. This suggested that the combined effect of CDs and SEO on the biodegradability of the nanocomposite was complex and dependent on their relative concentrations and interactions.

The results of the disk diffusion test showed that all four samples had antibacterial properties against E. coli and S. aureus (
Fig. 7 ). The CMC/SPA/SEO sample had the highest diameter of the non-growth halo against E. coli (26 mm) and S. aureus (18 mm). The CMC/SPA/CD/SEO sample also showed a high diameter of the non-growth halo against E. coli (22 mm) and S. aureus (15 mm). The CMC/SPA and CMC/SPA/CD samples had lower diameters of the non-growth halo against both bacteria. Based on the results, it seems that the CDs have a slight effect on the antibacterial property of the nanocomposite, as the CMC/SPA/CD sample had a slightly larger inhibition zone than the CMC/SPA sample. SEO has a high content of carvacrol, known to have antimicrobial effects against various pathogens [23]. SEO may synergize with the carboxymethyl cellulose and sodium polyacrylate, disrupting the cell membrane and inhibiting bacterial growth. The results are consistent with previous studies that reported the antibacterial activity of SEO against various microorganisms [22,45].

This study successfully synthesized and characterized innovative SANs by integrating CMC and SPA as the primary polymer matrix with CDs and SEO as functional additives. The results demonstrated that the inclusion of CDs significantly enhanced the water absorption capacity and mechanical strength of the SANs, with the CMC/SPA/CD sample achieving a remarkable water absorption capacity of 8621 % and a tensile strength of 4.6 MPa. Moreover, the addition of SEO imparted strong antibacterial properties, with inhibition zones reaching up to 26 mm against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus, while also improving the flexibility of the material. The combination of these components resulted in a multifunctional nanocomposite that not only meets the demands of high absorption and retention but also offers mechanical robustness and antimicrobial activity. The unique properties of these SANs make them highly suitable for real-world leachate management practices. Unlike conventional methods, which are often characterized by high costs, low efficiency, and secondary pollution, the SANs provide a economical, efficient, and environmentally friendly alternative. Their high-water absorption capacity ensures effective containment and management of landfill leachate, reducing the risk of groundwater contamination and surface water pollution. Additionally, the mechanical robustness and flexibility of the SANs allow them to maintain their structural integrity under varying environmental conditions, which is a significant advantage over existing nanocomposites that may degrade or lose functionality over time. The integration of plant-derived CDs and SEO in the SANs not only enhances their functional properties but also aligns with the principles of chemistry, promoting sustainability in waste management practices. The use of renewable, plant-based materials reduces reliance on synthetic chemicals and minimizes environmental impact, contributing to a more sustainable approach to managing landfill leachate. Moreover, the incorporation of SEO provides additional benefits, such as odor control and antimicrobial activity, which are crucial for maintaining sanitary conditions and reducing the spread of harmful bacteria. Economically, adopting these SANs on a larger scale could lead to significant cost savings in waste management practices. The materials used in the SANs are readily available and relatively inexpensive, and the manufacturing process is straightforward and scalable. This could result in lower production costs compared to more complex, synthetic nanocomposites, making the SANs an economically viable option for widespread use. Additionally, the long-term stability and biodegradability of these materials reduce the need for frequent replacement and minimize disposal costs, further enhancing their economic attractiveness. In conclusion, the SANs developed in this study offer a promising solution for advanced leachate management strategies, combining high absorption capacity, mechanical strength, and antimicrobial activity with environmental sustainability and economic feasibility. Future research should focus on optimizing the composition of these SANs to further enhance their performance and exploring their application in other environmental and industrial contexts. Additionally, investigating the long-term stability and biodegradability of these materials under various environmental conditions will be crucial for their broader adoption in waste management practices.

Manouchehr Behzadi: Validation, Investigation. Setareh Hosseinzadeh-Moghaddam: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation. Reza Monfared-Hajishirkiaee: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ali Rezaei: Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation. S. Shirin Shahangian: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors state that they do not have any competing financial interests or personal relationships that could potentially sway the integrity of the research detailed in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their utmost gratitude to Polyean Technology Inc for their invaluable financial and technical support in facilitating this groundbreaking research. Their contribution will undoubtedly accelerate knowledge development and pave the way for solutions to important challenges. Also, the authors express their gratitude to the University of Guilan for the support of this project.
